Q1: I tried to be very disciplined with my study routine, dedicating around 25 hours each week spread across mornings and evenings. Since I don't have reliable internet at home, I would plan my LMS sessions carefully when I could access wifi at the university library or a friend's place. I kept detailed handwritten notes and schedules because I couldn't always be online when I wanted to study. It was challenging but I made it work by being organized about when I could access materials versus when I could study them offline.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on when I could get internet access and when deadlines were approaching. Some weeks I would front-load my online time to download everything I needed, then study consistently throughout the week offline. Other times, competing responsibilities from work and family would push my studying to late nights before deadlines. I tried to maintain regularity but life at 28 with limited resources doesn't always cooperate with ideal study schedules.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew I needed to download new materials, check for announcements, or participate in discussions. I also logged in specifically to watch lecture videos since I couldn't easily download them. Sometimes the anxiety about missing something important would prompt me to check even when I didn't have specific tasks. The discussion forums were something I genuinely enjoyed participating in, so that was often a motivator too.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks I developed a rhythm where I would visit the library every Monday and Thursday to do my LMS activities. It became almost automatic - I would grab my spot, log in, download what I needed, participate in discussions, and watch videos. The routine helped manage my anxiety because I knew exactly when I would be connected. Having that structure made the unpredictability of my situation feel more manageable.
Q5: The discussion forums were incredibly valuable to me because I could learn from other students' perspectives and feel connected to the class community. The lecture videos were essential since I could watch them multiple times to really understand concepts. I also found the reading materials useful because I could download PDFs and study them offline at my own pace. These resources let me engage deeply even with my connectivity limitations.
Q6: I often delayed or minimally used the interactive quizzes and some of the supplementary video content because they required being online. The real-time elements were difficult for me to access consistently. I also sometimes ignored optional readings when I was overwhelmed, even though I knew they might help. My low assignment completion rate shows this - I understood the material better than my submissions reflected, but I couldn't always complete everything on time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see direct connections to the exam content or when it helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. The discussion posts felt valuable because they helped me process ideas by writing them out and getting peer feedback. I also valued anything I could take offline with me since my study time at home was precious. If something felt like busywork without clear learning outcomes, my anxiety made it hard to justify spending limited online time on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford given my time and connectivity constraints. Required activities got priority because I knew they affected my grade directly. But honestly, I sometimes wished I could engage more with optional materials because they often seemed interesting. The pressure of required work combined with my anxiety meant optional things got pushed aside even when I wanted to explore them.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I would download PDFs, lecture slides, and any materials I could save, then study them extensively at home using handwritten notes. I also used textbooks from the library and created my own summary sheets. My 25 hours of weekly study mostly happened away from any internet connection, working through problems and re-reading materials multiple times.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS would show nothing. I would spend hours working through downloaded problem sets at my kitchen table, or re-reading lecture notes on the bus to work. I discussed concepts with a study partner in person, which helped me understand things better. My best learning often happened when I was completely disconnected, processing information through writing and re-writing notes.
Q11: Yes, this was my primary mode of studying. I would access materials once during my library sessions, download everything available, and then work with those materials for days offline. A single video might be watched once online but I would review my notes from it multiple times at home. My LMS data probably shows minimal video time compared to how much I actually engaged with that content through my notes.
Q12: Around mid-semester, my anxiety spiked significantly and it became harder to engage. I was working more hours to make ends meet, and the gap between my understanding and my assignment completion was growing. I felt surprised by how quickly things accumulated when I fell behind. The mindfulness intervention helped somewhat, but my resilience was already wearing thin from juggling everything.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I would log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between completing an assignment properly or just submitting something incomplete. The confusion from missing real-time discussions made some topics harder to grasp later. My fatigue affected the quality of my engagement even when I was technically logged in and active.
Q14: Yes, there were times I would watch videos or scroll through materials while my mind was elsewhere, worrying about bills or work schedules. I might be logged in for an hour but only truly engaged for twenty minutes. My anxiety sometimes made it hard to concentrate even when I had the opportunity to study. The LMS would show activity, but my brain wasn't fully present.
Q15: I postponed assignments frequently even knowing they were important, which is why my completion rate was so low despite understanding the material. The anxiety was paralyzing sometimes - I would know exactly what needed to be done but feel overwhelmed about starting. I also avoided some difficult topics hoping they would make more sense later, which wasn't always a good strategy. My exam score being low despite my effort shows how this avoidance affected my outcomes.
Q16: Deadlines created intense bursts of LMS activity for me. I would log in more frequently as deadlines approached, sometimes frantically trying to submit work. They motivated me to prioritize certain tasks but also increased my anxiety significantly. I often wished I could spread my work more evenly, but my circumstances made deadline-driven behavior almost inevitable.
Q17: Feedback affected me deeply, sometimes too deeply. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Critical feedback sometimes triggered anxiety that made me avoid that type of task for a while. I appreciated detailed feedback because it helped me understand where to focus my limited study time. The delay in getting feedback sometimes meant I had already moved on before I could apply the lessons.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules helped me plan my offline study sessions effectively. I appreciated having materials organized in a predictable way so I could download systematically. However, the structure sometimes assumed constant connectivity that I didn't have. Weekly deadlines were challenging given my variable access to the internet throughout the week.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier for someone in my situation. The discussion forums being asynchronous meant I could participate during my limited online windows. However, any synchronous elements or activities requiring constant connectivity made engagement harder. The assumption that students could access materials anytime created barriers for me that others might not face.
Q20: I think I might be an example of this pattern. I logged in frequently, watched videos, participated in discussions, but my assignment completion was low. For me, it was the gap between understanding and producing - I engaged deeply with learning but struggled to complete formal submissions due to time constraints and anxiety. Sometimes engaging with content is easier than producing polished work under pressure.
Q21: I can see how that happens too. Some students might be very efficient learners who don't need to spend as much time in the LMS. They might use textbooks or external resources more. Or they might have better exam-taking skills than their participation suggests. For me, the opposite was true - my performance didn't reflect my engagement because of execution challenges rather than understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, time spent, and participation in trackable activities. They capture when students are connected and what they click on. But they miss the quality of attention, offline studying, emotional state during sessions, and external circumstances affecting engagement. My logs would show high engagement but wouldn't explain why my assignments and exam didn't match that engagement level.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and making consistent effort to learn, even when circumstances are difficult. It's about showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. For me, engagement meant spending hours with downloaded materials, participating thoughtfully in discussions, and trying to connect concepts even when I was anxious. It's an internal commitment that external metrics can only partially capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline studying, the anxiety I battled while trying to learn, or the economic pressures that affected my performance. It wouldn't capture the discussions with my study partner or the hours spent with handwritten notes. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that my failed grade doesn't reflect my genuine engagement with and understanding of the course material - it reflects my circumstances and struggles with execution under pressure.
